# simplequad

> TypeScript quadtree with SAT collision detection and MTV output. Zero dependencies. Built for 2D canvas games and interactive apps.

simplequad combines quadtree spatial indexing, Separating Axis Theorem (SAT) intersection testing, and Minimum Translation Vector (MTV) output in a single zero-dependency TypeScript package. One `query()` call returns both the colliding objects and the overlap vectors needed to push them apart — no second library, no glue code.

Supported shapes: BoundingBox (AABB), Circle, Point — freely mixed in the same tree.

## Installation

```
npm install simplequad
```

Or via CDN (UMD global `SimpleQuad`):

```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/simplequad@latest/dist/simplequad.umd.js"></script>
```

## Key API

### createQuadTree(bounds, capacity?, maxDepth?)

Creates a quadtree over the given bounding box.

- `bounds: BoundingBox` — the spatial region to manage (e.g. `{ x: 0, y: 0, width: 800, height: 600 }`)
- `capacity?: number` — objects per node before subdividing (default: 5)
- `maxDepth?: number` — max subdivision depth (default: 8)

Returns a `QuadTree<T>` where `T extends Bound`.

### QuadTree<T> methods

- `add(object: T): boolean` — add an object; returns false if it falls outside bounds
- `remove(object: T): boolean` — remove a specific object by reference
- `clear(): void` — remove all objects and reset all subdivisions
- `query(bounds: Bound): Array<QueryResult<T>>` — find all intersecting objects with MTVs; the query object itself is excluded
- `getData(): T[]` — flat deduplicated array of all objects currently in the tree

### Types

```typescript
type Bound = BoundingBox | Circle | Point;

interface Point { x: number; y: number; }
interface BoundingBox extends Point { width: number; height: number; }
interface Circle extends Point { r: number; }

interface QueryResult<T> {
  object: T;
  mtv: MinimumTranslationVectorInfo;
}

interface MinimumTranslationVectorInfo {
  vector: Point;     // apply to querying object's position to resolve overlap
  direction: Point;  // unit vector pointing from found object toward querying object
  magnitude: number; // overlap depth
}
```

Objects added to the tree must embed the relevant fields directly (`x`, `y`, and `width`/`height` for AABB, or `r` for Circle).

## Game Loop Pattern (recommended for moving objects)

Clear and rebuild the tree each frame — this is the pattern the benchmarks are based on:

```typescript
import { createQuadTree } from 'simplequad';

const tree = createQuadTree({ x: 0, y: 0, width: 800, height: 600 });

function gameLoop(entities) {
  tree.clear();
  for (const entity of entities) {
    tree.add(entity);
  }
  for (const entity of entities) {
    const hits = tree.query(entity);
    for (const { object, mtv } of hits) {
      // push entity out of overlap
      entity.x += mtv.vector.x;
      entity.y += mtv.vector.y;
    }
  }
}
```

## Moving a single object

simplequad does not track movement. To reposition an object:

```typescript
tree.remove(entity);
entity.x = newX;
entity.y = newY;
tree.add(entity);
```

## Performance

Benchmarked with AABB objects, clear+rebuild pattern, averaged across seeds:

| Objects | avg frame time | p95 frame time | 60fps budget used |
|---------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 100     | 0.14ms         | 0.15ms         | 0.8%              |
| 200     | 0.32ms         | 0.33ms         | 1.9%              |
| 300     | 0.56ms         | 0.59ms         | 3.4%              |
| 500     | 1.12ms         | 1.15ms         | 6.7%              |
| 1000    | 2.98ms         | 3.3ms          | 17.8%             |

Circle scenes run ~1.5–2× slower than AABB-only at the same object count, due to additional geometry in circle SAT.

## Fits well for

| Genre | Typical active objects | Fits? |
|-------|------------------------|-------|
| Casual / puzzle | < 100 | Comfortably |
| Platformer | 50–80 | Comfortably |
| Top-down shooter | 100–200 | Yes |
| Tower defense (peak wave) | 150–350 | Yes |
| Bullet hell | 500–2000 | No |
| RTS (large armies) | 300–1000+ | No |

## When to use something else

- **Beyond ~1000 dynamic objects** — look at purpose-built high-count spatial indexes
- **Rotation or convex polygons** — only axis-aligned shapes are supported
- **Continuous collision detection** — fast-moving objects can tunnel through thin geometry between frames
- **Full physics simulation** — simplequad provides collision detection and MTV, not mass, inertia, or constraints

## Links

- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/simplequad
- GitHub: https://github.com/rcasto/simplequad
- Live examples: https://rcasto.github.io/simplequad/
- Source (TypeScript): https://github.com/rcasto/simplequad/tree/master/src
